exercises.title: What SQL does JpaRepository.findAll() generate?
exercises.scenario: JpaRepository translates Java calls to SQL automatically.
exercises.expected_solution:
INSERT INTO order (status, amount, id) VALUES (?,?,?)
SELECT ... FROM order WHERE id=?
SELECT ... FROM order
DELETE FROM order WHERE id=?
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM order

test_cases.expected:
INSERT INTO order (status, amount, id) VALUES (?,?,?)
SELECT ... FROM order WHERE id=?
SELECT ... FROM order
DELETE FROM order WHERE id=?
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM order

hints: save() → INSERT (new) or UPDATE (existing)
findById() → SELECT WHERE id = ?
count() → SELECT COUNT(*)

exercises.title: Complete the @Entity class
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make this a valid JPA entity.

blanks.hint: Marks class as a JPA-managed table
The no-arg constructor must match the class name

hints: @Entity + @Table: declare this class as a JPA entity
@Id: the primary key
protected no-arg constructor: Hibernate requirement

exercises.title: Minimal @Entity and JpaRepository
exercises.scenario: The simplest possible JPA entity + repository.
exercises.instructions: Write a minimal @Entity `StockItem` (fields: id String, symbol String, qty int) and its `StockItemRepository` extending JpaRepository. Include the no-arg constructor.
hints: @Id @GeneratedValue on the id field
protected no-arg constructor is mandatory
JpaRepository<EntityType, IdType>

exercises.title: Derived query methods — name parsing
exercises.scenario: Spring generates SQL from method names.
exercises.instructions:
Add 5 derived query methods to `ProductRepository`:
1. Find products by category
2. Find products cheaper than a max price
3. Find products whose name contains a search string (case-insensitive)
4. Check if a product with a given name exists
5. Count products by category
hints: findBy + FieldName → WHERE field = ?
LessThan → <, ContainingIgnoreCase → LIKE %?% case-insensitive
existsBy returns boolean; countBy returns long

exercises.title: @DataJpaTest — test save and findById
exercises.scenario: @DataJpaTest loads H2 + JPA only — fast repository testing.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @DataJpaTest for `StockItemRepository`:
1. Save a StockItem → id is assigned
2. findById returns the saved item
3. findById for non-existent id → empty Optional
hints: @DataJpaTest loads H2 + JPA in-memory, no full Spring context
@Autowired StockItemRepository — Spring injects it
Each test runs in a rollback transaction — clean state

exercises.title: @Column customization
exercises.scenario: @Column lets you customize the database column.
exercises.instructions:
Write an @Entity `Employee` with:
- `id` (UUID, auto-generated)
- `fullName` → column 'full_name', not null, max 100 chars
- `email` → unique, not null
- `salary` → not null, column 'monthly_salary'
- `active` → default true (handled in Java constructor)
- Required no-arg constructor and all-field constructor
hints: @Column(name='col_name', nullable=false, length=100, unique=true)
active=true in field declaration: Java default, not DB constraint
No @Column on id: @Id + @GeneratedValue handles it

exercises.title: @DataJpaTest — test derived queries
exercises.scenario: Test that derived query methods return the right data.
exercises.instructions:
Write @DataJpaTest tests for `ProductRepository`:
1. findByCategory('Electronics') returns 2 of 3 saved products
2. findByPriceLessThan(1000) returns only the cheap product
3. findByNameContainingIgnoreCase('mouse') finds case-insensitively
4. existsByName returns true/false correctly
hints: @BeforeEach seed() adds test data — each test gets a fresh transaction
@DataJpaTest rolls back each test — seed() re-runs before each
findByPriceLessThan(1000): only Mouse at 799 qualifies

exercises.title: @Query — custom JPQL
exercises.scenario: @Query writes JPQL when derived names aren't expressive enough.
exercises.instructions:
Add two @Query methods to `OrderRepository`:
1. Find orders by userId with amount above a threshold (JPQL)
2. Find total amount by userId (JPQL aggregation returning Double)

Test both with @DataJpaTest.
hints: @Query uses JPQL: entity names (Order), field names (o.amount), not table/column names
@Param('uid') links :uid placeholder to method parameter
SUM returns null if no rows — return type Double (nullable)

exercises.title: @Transactional service with JPA
exercises.instructions:
Write `TransferService.transfer(fromId, toId, amount)` that:
1. Loads both accounts from AccountRepository
2. Throws if either doesn't exist or insufficient balance
3. Deducts from sender, credits receiver, saves both
4. If any step fails, whole transaction rolls back

Annotate correctly. Test with @DataJpaTest.
hints: @DataJpaTest + @Import(TransferService.class): import service bean into slice test
@Transactional on service: both saves in same transaction
Insufficient funds: debit throws → transaction rolls back

exercises.title: JPQL vs SQL — field names vs column names
exercises.scenario: JPQL uses Java entity field names, not database column names.
exercises.expected_solution:
Correct: B and D
Wrong: A (uses column name full_name, not field name fullName)
Wrong: C (native SQL syntax without nativeQuery=true; also uses column name)

test_cases.expected:
Correct: B and D
Wrong: A (uses column name full_name, not field name fullName)
Wrong: C (native SQL syntax without nativeQuery=true; also uses column name)

hints: JPQL: use Java field names (fullName, department)
@Column(name='full_name') changes the column but NOT the JPQL name
Native SQL: needs nativeQuery=true AND uses column names

exercises.title: Complete product CRUD with JPA
exercises.scenario: Full CRUD service backed by JPA repository.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete `ProductService` (@Service @Transactional) with JPA:
- create(name, price, category): throws if name exists
- findById(id): returns Optional
- findAll(): returns all
- update(id, name, price): throws if not found
- delete(id): throws if not found
- search(query): findByNameContainingIgnoreCase

Test all 6 operations with @DataJpaTest + @Import.
hints: @DataJpaTest + @Import(ProductService.class): service needs to be imported
create duplicate: existsByName returns true → throw
update: load entity, mutate fields, save → Hibernate detects changes

exercises.title: Complex derived queries with sorting and limits
exercises.scenario: Spring Data supports ordering and limiting in method names.
exercises.instructions:
Add 4 complex derived queries to `OrderRepository`:
1. Find top 5 orders by amount descending for a userId
2. Find all COMPLETED orders after a date, sorted by date desc
3. Count orders by userId and status
4. Find orders with amount between min and max

Test at least 2 with @DataJpaTest.
hints: findTop5By: LIMIT 5; OrderByAmountDesc: ORDER BY amount DESC
Between: BETWEEN ? AND ? (inclusive on both ends)
countByUserIdAndStatus: COUNT WHERE user_id=? AND status=?

exercises.title: Full REST + JPA — products CRUD endpoint
exercises.scenario: Connect the REST controller from T13.2 to real JPA persistence.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete stack: @Entity Product + ProductRepository + ProductService (@Transactional) + ProductController (@RestController). Test with @DataJpaTest for the repository layer.

Endpoints:
- GET /api/products → 200 list
- GET /api/products/{id} → 200 or 404
- POST /api/products → 201
- DELETE /api/products/{id} → 204 or 404
exercises.starter_code:
// Full stack: Entity → Repository → Service → Controller
// + @DataJpaTest for repository
hints: Controller calls Service; Service calls Repository — layer by layer
@Transactional on Service: all repo calls in one transaction
@DataJpaTest tests only the repository layer

exercises.title: application.properties — full JPA configuration
exercises.scenario: Configure H2, Hibernate, logging, and schema creation.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete `application.properties` for a dev environment:
- H2 in-memory database enabled with console
- Schema: create-drop
- Show SQL: true, formatted
- JPA dialect: H2
- App name: inventory-service, port: 8090
- Two custom properties: inventory.low-stock=5, inventory.max-items=1000

Then write a @Service that reads both custom properties and a @DataJpaTest showing H2 is active.
hints: spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:dbname
spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create-drop: tables created on start, dropped on stop
@DataJpaTest auto-configures H2 — no application.properties needed for tests

exercises.title: Zerodha-style portfolio tracker — JPA synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build a `PortfolioService` with full JPA backing:
- `Holding` @Entity: id, userId, symbol, qty, avgBuyPrice
- `HoldingRepository`: findByUserId, findByUserIdAndSymbol, sumQtyByUserIdAndSymbol (@Query)
- `PortfolioService` (@Service @Transactional): addHolding, getPortfolio(userId), getTotalValue(userId, Map<symbol,currentPrice>), removeHolding
- 5 @DataJpaTest tests

All tests must pass.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete Zerodha-style portfolio tracker with JPA
hints: @DataJpaTest + @Import(PortfolioService.class): both beans available
sumQty @Query: SUM() returns null if no rows — use Integer not int
getTotalValue: stream holdings, price from map or fallback to avgBuyPrice
